---
title: "NKX2-2 as a Prognostic Biomarker in Ewing Sarcoma"
subtitle: "Survival Analysis of an Independent Cohort (GSE63157)"
author: "Musfirah"
date: "17/07/2026"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    theme: flatly
    code_folding: hide
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE,
                       fig.width = 8, fig.height = 6)
library(dplyr)
library(GEOquery)
library(Biobase)
library(survival)
library(survminer)
```

# 1. Introduction

NKX2-2 was previously identified in our DESeq2 differential expression analysis
(Ewing sarcoma vs. liposarcoma, GSE2553) as a top upregulated gene and a known
diagnostic marker of Ewing sarcoma. Diagnostic value, however, does not
automatically imply prognostic value — a marker that distinguishes tumor types
is a different biological question from a marker that predicts patient
outcome within a single tumor type. This report tests whether NKX2-2
expression level is associated with survival outcomes in an independent
Ewing sarcoma cohort.

**Dataset:** GSE63157 — Children's Oncology Group (COG) cohort of localized
Ewing sarcoma patients, diagnosed after 1994, treated under COG protocols
(AEWS0031, INT-0154). This dataset has been independently used as validation
data in multiple peer-reviewed prognostic studies, supporting confidence in
its clinical annotation.

**Endpoints analyzed:**

- **Overall Survival (OS)** — time to death from any cause
- **Event-Free Survival (EFS)** — time to relapse or death

# 2. Methods

## 2.1 Data acquisition and cleaning

Clinical and expression data were downloaded via `GEOquery::getGEO()`.
Survival status/time fields were extracted from sample characteristics and
recoded to standard 0/1 event conventions (Alive/No event = 0, Dead/Relapse
= 1). One sample with a Second Malignant Neoplasm (SMN) event was excluded
from the EFS analysis, as this represents a competing risk rather than a
relapse of the primary tumor, and standard KM/Cox methods are not designed
to handle competing risks without explicit modeling.

## 2.2 Gene expression mapping

GSE63157 was profiled on an Affymetrix Human Exon 1.0 ST array (GPL5175),
which annotates probes via a compound `gene_assignment` text field rather
than a direct gene symbol column. NKX2-2 was mapped by pattern-matching
this field; a single unambiguous probe (ID 3900545) was identified.

## 2.3 Statistical analysis

Patients were divided into High/Low expression groups by median split.
Kaplan-Meier curves were compared using the log-rank test. Cox proportional
hazards regression was used to estimate hazard ratios for continuous
expression, both unadjusted and adjusted for sex and tumor site. The
proportional hazards assumption was tested via `cox.zph()`; where violated,
models were refit stratified by the offending covariate. Tumor site was
collapsed to a 2-level variable (Extremity vs. Axial/Other) due to small
cell sizes in the original 3-level classification (Pelvis, n=5-6).

```{r load-data}
gse <- getGEO("GSE63157", GSEMatrix = TRUE, destdir = "~/bioinformatics/data/")
eset <- gse[[1]]
expr <- exprs(eset)
fdata <- fData(eset)

nkx_probes <- rownames(fdata)[grepl("// NKX2-2 //", fdata$gene_assignment, fixed = TRUE)]
nkx_expr <- expr[nkx_probes, ]

expr_df <- data.frame(sample_id = names(nkx_expr),
                       NKX2_2_expression = as.numeric(nkx_expr))

os_df  <- read.csv("~/bioinformatics/results/GSE63157/GSE63157_OS_clean.csv",  stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
efs_df <- read.csv("~/bioinformatics/results/GSE63157/GSE63157_EFS_clean.csv", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

os_df  <- os_df  %>% inner_join(expr_df, by = "sample_id")
efs_df <- efs_df %>% inner_join(expr_df, by = "sample_id")

os_df$NKX2_2_group  <- ifelse(os_df$NKX2_2_expression  > median(os_df$NKX2_2_expression),  "High", "Low")
efs_df$NKX2_2_group <- ifelse(efs_df$NKX2_2_expression > median(efs_df$NKX2_2_expression), "High", "Low")

os_df$sex   <- factor(os_df$sex)
efs_df$sex  <- factor(efs_df$sex)
os_df$tumor_site_simple  <- factor(ifelse(os_df$tumor_site   == "Extremity", "Extremity", "Axial_Other"))
efs_df$tumor_site_simple <- factor(ifelse(efs_df$tumor_site  == "Extremity", "Extremity", "Axial_Other"))
```

**Cohort sizes:** `r nrow(os_df)` patients with OS data (`r sum(os_df$os_event)` deaths),
`r nrow(efs_df)` patients with EFS data (`r sum(efs_df$efs_event)` relapses).

# 3. Results

## 3.1 Kaplan-Meier Survival Curves

### Overall Survival

```{r km-os}
os_surv <- Surv(time = os_df$os_time, event = os_df$os_event)
os_fit  <- survfit(os_surv ~ NKX2_2_group, data = os_df)

ggsurvplot(os_fit, data = os_df, pval = TRUE, risk.table = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE,
           palette = c("#E7B800", "#2E9FDF"),
           xlab = "Time (days)", ylab = "Overall Survival Probability",
           title = "NKX2-2 Expression vs Overall Survival",
           legend.title = "NKX2-2", legend.labs = c("High", "Low"))
```

### Event-Free Survival

```{r km-efs}
efs_surv <- Surv(time = efs_df$efs_time, event = efs_df$efs_event)
efs_fit  <- survfit(efs_surv ~ NKX2_2_group, data = efs_df)

ggsurvplot(efs_fit, data = efs_df, pval = TRUE, risk.table = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE,
           palette = c("#E7B800", "#2E9FDF"),
           xlab = "Time (days)", ylab = "Event-Free Survival Probability",
           title = "NKX2-2 Expression vs Event-Free Survival",
           legend.title = "NKX2-2", legend.labs = c("High", "Low"))
```

Low NKX2-2 expression was associated with significantly worse event-free
survival (log-rank p = 0.037) and a non-significant trend toward worse
overall survival (log-rank p = 0.067).

## 3.2 Cox Proportional Hazards Regression

```{r cox-models}
cox_os  <- coxph(os_surv  ~ NKX2_2_expression + tumor_site_simple + strata(sex), data = os_df)
cox_efs <- coxph(efs_surv ~ NKX2_2_expression + tumor_site_simple + strata(sex), data = efs_df)

summary(cox_os)
summary(cox_efs)
```

**Proportional hazards assumption check:**

```{r ph-check}
cox.zph(cox_os)
cox.zph(cox_efs)
```

All covariates satisfy the proportional hazards assumption in the
sex-stratified models (all p > 0.05), confirming model validity. Note that
an earlier unstratified model showed a PH violation for sex (OS: p=0.045,
EFS: p=0.016); stratifying by sex resolved this while preserving stable
hazard ratio estimates for NKX2-2.

**Summary of NKX2-2 hazard ratios across model specifications:**

| Model | HR | 95% CI | p-value |
|---|---|---|---|
| OS univariate | 0.82 | 0.65–1.03 | 0.093 |
| OS multivariate (stratified) | 0.82 | 0.64–1.06 | 0.125 |
| EFS univariate | 0.85 | 0.68–1.08 | 0.181 |
| EFS multivariate (stratified) | 0.83 | 0.65–1.07 | 0.145 |

# 4. Discussion

Across every model specification tested, higher NKX2-2 expression was
consistently associated with reduced risk (HR range 0.82–0.85), and this
association reached statistical significance at the group level for
event-free survival (log-rank p = 0.037). The continuous Cox models did not
reach conventional significance individually, most plausibly reflecting
limited statistical power (14–17 events) rather than an absent effect,
given the consistency of the hazard ratio across five independent model
configurations and reasonably strong concordance indices (0.77 for OS,
0.69 for EFS).

This finding adds a prognostic dimension to NKX2-2's established diagnostic
role in Ewing sarcoma. Loss of NKX2-2 expression — a known EWS-FLI1 target
gene central to the Ewing sarcoma differentiation program — being linked to
worse outcome is consistent with existing literature describing NKX2-2 loss
alongside a less-differentiated, more aggressive phenotype.

**Limitations:**

- Modest sample size (n=45-46) limits power for continuous-variable Cox models
- Median-split analysis, while standard practice, can detect group-level
  differences that a linear continuous model underpowers if the true
  relationship is non-linear or threshold-like
- Tumor site was collapsed due to a small Pelvis subgroup (n=5-6); this may
  obscure real site-specific effects
- Single-cohort validation; independent replication in an additional dataset
  (e.g., TCGA-SARC) would strengthen these findings

# 5. Conclusion

NKX2-2 expression shows a consistent, directionally stable association with
survival outcomes in Ewing sarcoma, reaching statistical significance for
event-free survival in this independent validation cohort. These results
support further investigation of NKX2-2 as a prognostic — not merely
diagnostic — biomarker in Ewing sarcoma.
